XII. The Issuer's Equity Incentive and Other Institutional Arrangements and Their Implementation

As of the date of signing of this Prospectus, the Issuer's direct employee shareholding platform is Hefei Jixin (合肥集鑫), which directly holds 8.37% of the Issuer's shares. The Issuer does not have any employee stock option plans formulated prior to the initial public offering application and to be implemented after listing.

(I) First Phase Employee Shareholding Plan

1. Implementation Procedures

On December 4, 2020, the Company convened a shareholders' meeting and reviewed and approved the relevant proposals concerning the sources of employee incentives. On February 3, 2021, the Company convened a board of directors meeting and reviewed and approved the relevant proposals concerning the First Phase Employee Shareholding Plan. On November 27, 2021, the Company convened a board of directors meeting and reviewed and approved the relevant proposals concerning amendments to the First Phase Employee Shareholding Plan.

2. Basic Information and Composition of Personnel of the Employee Shareholding Platform

The incentive recipients under the Company's First Phase Employee Shareholding Plan primarily include management talent, business backbone personnel, and core employees who possess a sense of mission, responsibility, and identification with the Company's medium- and long-term development strategy, as well as other employees who have made outstanding contributions to the Company.

In July 2020, the Company established Hefei Jixin (合肥集鑫) as a direct employee shareholding platform. Due to the large number of incentive recipients, the Company established Hefei Jixin No. 0 Enterprise Management Partnership (Limited Partnership) (合肥集鑫零号企业管理合伙企业（有限合伙）) and Hong Kong Jixin Limited (香港集鑫有限公司) as limited partners of Hefei Jixin, and further established several shareholding platforms above them. Incentive recipients indirectly hold shares of the Company by holding corresponding shares in the respective shareholding platforms. For details of the above employee shareholding platforms, please refer to "Section XII: Appendices" — "X. Employee Shareholding Platforms" of this Prospectus.

3. Lock-up Period Arrangements

Pursuant to the Employee Shareholding Plan Management Measures, the incentive recipients and shareholding platforms have committed to locking up their incentive shares until the expiration of the lock-up period stipulated by laws and regulations after the Company's listing. Without the consent of the Employee Shareholding Plan Management Committee, they may not sell, transfer, pledge, mortgage, set up other guarantees or encumbrances, repay debts, donate, or otherwise dispose of the incentive shares they hold, or withdraw from the First Phase Employee Shareholding Plan in any manner. During the lock-up period before and after the Company's listing, incentive recipients who wish to transfer and exit their incentive shares may only transfer such shares to employees within the First Phase Employee Shareholding Plan or other qualified employees in accordance with the Employee Shareholding Plan Management Measures.

The employee shareholding platform Hefei Jixin (合肥集鑫) has signed a letter of commitment regarding the lock-up of shares of the Company held prior to this offering, committing that within 36 months from the date on which the Issuer's shares are listed on the stock exchange, it will not transfer or entrust others to manage the shares of the Issuer already issued prior to the initial public offering that it holds, nor will it have the Issuer repurchase such shares. For details of the commitments by the employee shareholding platform Hefei Jixin regarding the lock-up period and reduction of shareholding in the Issuer's shares, please refer to "Section XII: Appendices" — "V. Commitments Related to Investor Protection" — "(I) Commitments Regarding Lock-up Arrangements for Shares Held by Shareholders Prior to This Offering, Voluntary Lock-up of Shares, Extension of Lock-up Periods, and Shareholders' Shareholding and Reduction Intentions, etc." — "1. Commitments Regarding Lock-up Arrangements for Shares Held in the Company, Voluntary Lock-up of Shares, and Extension of Lock-up Periods" of this Prospectus.

4. Treatment of Shares Upon Employee Departure

If an incentive recipient leaves employment during the implementation of the First Phase Employee Shareholding Plan due to the occurrence or existence of "red line conduct" as defined in the Employee Shareholding Plan Management Measures, the incentive recipient shall withdraw from the First Phase Employee Shareholding Plan by means of partnership withdrawal, share transfer, or other methods. For departures due to reasons other than "red line conduct," the Management Committee permits the incentive recipient to continue holding the incentive shares under the First Phase Employee Shareholding Plan.

(II) Second Phase Employee Shareholding Plan

1. Implementation Procedures

On March 2, 2023, the Company convened a shareholders' meeting and reviewed and approved the relevant proposals concerning the sources of employee incentives. On June 9, 2023, the Company convened a board of directors meeting and reviewed and approved the relevant proposals concerning the Second Phase Employee Shareholding Plan.

2. Basic Information and Composition of Personnel of the Employee Shareholding Platform

The incentive recipients under the Second Phase Employee Shareholding Plan primarily include: senior management personnel; outstanding middle-level management and core technical personnel, technical and business backbone personnel; and high-potential technical personnel who strongly identify with the Group's corporate culture and development strategy.

To implement the Company's Second Phase Employee Shareholding Plan, the Company established Hefei Jixin No. 40 Enterprise Management Partnership (Limited Partnership) (合肥集鑫肆拾号企业管理合伙企业（有限合伙）) as a limited partner of Hefei Jixin, and further established several shareholding platforms above it. Incentive recipients indirectly hold shares of the Company by holding corresponding shares in the respective shareholding platforms. For details of the above employee shareholding platforms, please refer to "Section XII: Appendices" — "X. Employee Shareholding Platforms" of this Prospectus.

3. Lock-up Period Arrangements

(1) General Lock-up Period Arrangements

The incentive shares granted to incentive recipients pursuant to the Second Phase Employee Shareholding Plan and the Employee Shareholding Plan Management Measures shall be locked up for 60 months from the equity grant date specified in the grant agreement documents. Unlocking shall occur every 12 months (each such period being an "Unlocking Cycle") based on the incentive recipient's performance evaluation results and other performance. During each Unlocking Cycle, each incentive recipient may unlock a maximum of 20% of the total incentive shares held by such recipient.

Pursuant to the Employee Shareholding Plan Management Measures, the incentive recipients and shareholding platforms have committed to locking up their incentive shares until the expiration of 36 months after the Company's listing. Without the consent of the Employee Shareholding Plan Management Committee, they may not sell, transfer, pledge, mortgage, set up other guarantees or encumbrances, repay debts, donate, or otherwise dispose of the incentive shares they hold, or withdraw from the Second Phase Employee Shareholding Plan in any manner. During the lock-up period before and after the Company's listing, incentive recipients who wish to transfer and exit their incentive shares may only transfer such shares to employees within the Second Phase Employee Shareholding Plan or other qualified employees in accordance with the Employee Shareholding Plan Management Measures.

The employee shareholding platform Hefei Jixin (合肥集鑫) has signed a letter of commitment regarding the lock-up of shares of the Company held prior to this offering, committing that within 36 months from the date on which the Issuer's shares are listed on the stock exchange, it will not transfer or entrust others to manage the shares of the Issuer already issued prior to the initial public offering that it holds, nor will it have the Issuer repurchase such shares. For details of the commitments by the employee shareholding platform Hefei Jixin regarding the lock-up period and reduction of shareholding in the Issuer's shares, please refer to "Section XII: Appendices" — "V. Commitments Related to Investor Protection" — "(I) Commitments Regarding Lock-up Arrangements for Shares Held by Shareholders Prior to This Offering, Voluntary Lock-up of Shares, Extension of Lock-up Periods, and Shareholders' Shareholding and Reduction Intentions, etc." — "1. Commitments Regarding Lock-up Arrangements for Shares Held in the Company, Voluntary Lock-up of Shares, and Extension of Lock-up Periods" of this Prospectus.

(2) Other Lock-up and Shareholding Reduction Arrangements

Pursuant to the provisions of the Company's Employee Shareholding Plan Shareholding Reduction Management Rules, incentive recipients meeting certain requirements regarding position, rank, reporting level, and number of shares held shall be designated as "First-Tier Incentive Recipients" or "Second-Tier Incentive Recipients" (collectively referred to as "Specific Incentive Recipients"). The Issuer's Chairman and other Specific Incentive Recipients have made the following commitments with respect to the incentive shares held by them under the Second Phase Employee Shareholding Plan:

The Issuer's Chairman Zhu Yiming (朱一明) has committed: Within 120 months from the date on which the Issuer's shares are listed on the stock exchange, he will not transfer or entrust others to manage the shares of the Issuer already issued prior to the initial public offering that he directly or indirectly holds, nor will he have the Issuer repurchase such shares. From the first reduction period after the expiration of the lock-up period, he may reduce his locked shares by no more than 20% of the total locked shares held by him as of the end of the previous year in any given year. After the lock-up period of ten years has expired, he may reduce all remaining locked shares at one time, unless laws, regulations, securities regulatory authorities, or stock exchanges provide for more stringent restrictions on reduction. Within 6 months after his resignation, he will not transfer any shares of the Issuer held by him.

First-Tier Incentive Recipients have committed: From the first reduction period after the expiration of the lock-up period, during their term of employment (including the year of departure), they may reduce their locked shares by no more than 20% of the total locked shares held by them as of the end of the previous year in any given year. After the lock-up period of ten years has expired, they may reduce all remaining incentive shares at one time, unless laws, regulations, the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC), or stock exchanges provide for more stringent restrictions on reduction. Such incentive recipients shall not reduce any incentive shares within 6 months after leaving the Company.

Second-Tier Incentive Recipients have committed: From the first reduction period after the expiration of the lock-up period, during their term of employment (including the year of departure), they may reduce their incentive shares by no more than 30% of the total locked shares held by them as of the end of the previous year in any given year. After the lock-up period of eight years has expired, they may reduce all remaining locked shares at one time, unless laws, regulations, the CSRC, or stock exchanges provide for more stringent restrictions on reduction.

For details of the commitments made by the Issuer's Chairman Zhu Yiming and other Specific Incentive Recipients regarding the lock-up arrangements, voluntary lock-up of shares, and extension of lock-up periods with respect to the incentive shares held by them under the Second Phase Employee Shareholding Plan, please refer to "Section XII: Appendices" — "V. Commitments Related to Investor Protection" — "(I) Commitments Regarding Lock-up Arrangements for Shares Held by Shareholders Prior to This Offering, Voluntary Lock-up of Shares, Extension of Lock-up Periods, and Shareholders' Shareholding and Reduction Intentions, etc." — "1. Commitments Regarding Lock-up Arrangements for Shares Held in the Company, Voluntary Lock-up of Shares, and Extension of Lock-up Periods" of this Prospectus.

4. Treatment of Shares Upon Employee Departure

When an employee's departure triggers a withdrawal event, if it does not involve "red line conduct" as defined in the Employee Shareholding Plan Management Measures, the unvested shareholding portions held by the relevant incentive recipient shall in principle be recovered based on the decision of the Employee Shareholding Plan Management Committee. If the departure is due to "red line conduct" as defined in the Employee Shareholding Plan Management Measures, the Management Committee shall decide to recover all vested and unvested shareholding portions held by the incentive recipient in their entirety.

(III) Impact on the Company's Operations, Financial Condition, and Control Structure

By implementing the above employee shareholding plans, the Company has established and improved a long-term incentive and restraint mechanism, fully mobilizing the enthusiasm of the Company's R&D and core management personnel. This is conducive to balancing the long-term interests of employees and the Company, laying a solid foundation for the Company's sound development, and has a positive impact on the Company's future financial condition and operating results. As a result of the implementation of the employee shareholding plans, the amounts of share-based compensation recognized by the Company in each period of the reporting period were RMB 84,273,200, RMB 2,082,503,300, RMB 2,322,516,200, and RMB 733,676,200, respectively. The share-based payment expenses have not had an adverse impact on the Company's financial condition. Before and after the implementation of the above employee shareholding plans, the Company's controlling shareholder and actual controller have not changed; therefore, the above employee shareholding plans will not affect the stability of the Company's control structure.

Section V: Business and Technology

I. The Issuer's Principal Business, Main Products, and Services

(I) The Company's Principal Business and Main Products

1. The Company's Principal Business

Chang Xin Technology (长鑫科技) is China's largest, most technologically advanced, and most comprehensively deployed integrated DRAM research, design, and manufacturing enterprise. Since its establishment in 2016, the Company has consistently focused on the research, design, production, and sale of DRAM products. The Company has adopted a "generational leapfrog R&D" strategy and has achieved mass production across process technology platforms from the first generation to the fourth generation, as well as product coverage and iterative upgrades from DDR4 and LPDDR4X to DDR5 and LPDDR5/5X. The Company's core products and process technologies have now reached internationally advanced levels. According to data from Omdia, measured by shipment volume, the Company has become the No. 1 DRAM manufacturer in China and the No. 4 DRAM manufacturer globally.

DRAM is an important cornerstone of modern information technology and the development of the digital information industry, and is widely used in data centers, mobile devices and terminals, communications, intelligent manufacturing, and other fields. In September 2019, the Company launched its independently designed and produced 8Gb DDR4 product, achieving a breakthrough "from zero to one" for the DRAM industry in mainland China. At the same time, the Company has actively seized industry development trends and continuously iterated its products. The Company has now formed a diversified product portfolio including the DDR series and LPDDR series, and can provide diversified product solutions including DRAM wafers, DRAM chips, and DRAM modules, effectively meeting the market demands of servers, mobile devices, personal computers, intelligent vehicles, and other markets.

China is one of the world's largest DRAM demand markets, while the world's top three DRAM manufacturers — Samsung Electronics, SK Hynix, and Micron Technology — have long accounted for more than 90% of the global market share. The Company is committed to continuously expanding its production capacity, steadily increasing its global market share, and providing a stable supply for China's DRAM market. The Company has a total of three 12-inch DRAM wafer fabrication plants in Hefei and Beijing, with production capacity ranking No. 1 in China and No. 4 globally.

The Company places great emphasis on technological innovation and advanced process R&D, has established a comprehensive R&D innovation system, built an experienced R&D team, and has undertaken numerous national major special project tasks. Through continuous R&D investment and technological innovation, the Company has produced numerous scientific research achievements and has built a comprehensive and complete core technology system across all business segments including DRAM product design, manufacturing processes, packaging and testing, and module design and applications. The main core technologies have reached internationally advanced levels. As of June 30, 2025, the Company holds a total of 3,116 domestic patents (of which 2,348 are invention patents) and 2,473 overseas patents. According to statistics from the World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO), the Company ranked 22nd globally in the number of international patent application publications in 2023. According to data published by IFI Claims Patent Services, a leading U.S. patent services institution, the Company ranked 42nd globally in U.S. patent grants in 2024, ranking fourth among all Chinese companies on the list.

As a leading enterprise in the DRAM industry, after years of development, the Company has established a mutually dependent, co-developing industrial ecosystem with its upstream and downstream partners. The Company has accumulated extensive high-quality customer resources across major sectors including servers, mobile devices, personal computers, and intelligent vehicles, and has established a strong brand effect. The Company has engaged in deep cooperation with core industry customers such as Alibaba Cloud, ByteDance, Tencent, Lenovo, Xiaomi, Transsion, Honor, OPPO, and vivo, and has continuously earned customer recognition and praise. In addition, the Company actively cooperates closely with semiconductor memory design firms, EDA vendors, material manufacturers, equipment and component manufacturers, memory module manufacturers, and others to jointly promote the development of China's DRAM market and the improvement of the industrial ecosystem. As the Company's existing production capacity is gradually released and its planned construction capacity is progressively completed, the Company will continue to increase its market share, empower the information society with memory technology, and become a technologically leading and commercially successful semiconductor memory enterprise.

2. The Company's Main Products and Services

The Company's products cover two mainstream series — DDR and LPDDR — and each series can provide currently mainstream fourth-generation and fifth-generation products, including DDR4, DDR5, LPDDR4X, and LPDDR5/5X. These products are widely used in markets including servers, mobile devices, personal computers, and intelligent vehicles. The Company can provide diversified product solutions including DRAM wafers, DRAM chips, and DRAM modules, tailored to the application characteristics of different products and the needs of different customers. Among these, DRAM chips are the primary product type shipped and sold by the Company during the reporting period. With a broad product portfolio and outstanding product performance, the Company is able to provide customers with comprehensive DRAM memory solutions. Details of the Company's main products and services are as follows:

(1) DRAM Chips

The Company's DRAM chips primarily include the DDR series and the LPDDR series. Specific details are as follows:

1) DDR Series
① DDR4

Leveraging long-term technological accumulation and innovation, the Company has launched independently designed, developed, and produced DDR4 chips. The Company's DDR4 chips possess multi-domain application support capabilities, can meet the needs of multiple product combination uses, and provide high-reliability assurance for data storage and high-speed transmission. Currently, in response to development trends in downstream application markets, the Company has launched new-generation products such as DDR5 through technology iteration. Since late 2024, Chang Xin Technology's own DDR4 products have ceased production.

② DDR5

With the development of new-generation information technology, the performance and system power consumption requirements for various types of terminal electronic devices continue to increase, and the demand for high-performance memory in downstream markets is growing rapidly. DDR5, as the new generation of DDR memory chips, is rapidly replacing DDR4 due to its higher bandwidth and lower power consumption, and is being applied in the mid-to-high-end market. Based on the current DRAM product iteration trend, the Company has launched DDR5 products and completed mass production, with product performance reaching internationally advanced levels. The products are widely used in servers, personal computers, and other fields, achieving broader downstream market coverage.

2) LPDDR
① LPDDR4X

LPDDR chips achieve low-power operation by reducing the wire resistance and channel width between the memory and the CPU, and are primarily used in portable devices such as smartphones and tablet computers. Compared to LPDDR4 chips, LPDDR4X chips have been optimized in terms of packaging methods, pin design, and interface design, achieving lower power consumption and better stability under the same performance and speed conditions. The Company's LPDDR4X chips combine the features of large capacity, high speed, high bandwidth, and low power consumption, providing a stable and smooth user experience. They have entered the supply chains of mainstream manufacturers including Xiaomi, OPPO, vivo, Transsion, and Lenovo, achieved widespread application in terminal products such as smartphones, and reached overseas markets through relevant terminal channels.

② LPDDR5/5X

The Company's independently designed, developed, and produced LPDDR5/5X chips feature large capacity, high speed, ultra-low power consumption, and high security. Compared to the previous generation LPDDR4X chips, the Company's LPDDR5/5X chips have significantly increased single-die capacity and speed, while further reducing power consumption. In addition, the Company's LPDDR5/5X chips incorporate powerful RAS (Reliability, Availability, and Serviceability) functionality and, through on-die ECC and other technologies, can achieve real-time error correction, effectively reducing system failures, ensuring data security, and enhancing system stability. The Company's LPDDR5/5X memory chips can effectively meet the market demands of mid-to-high-end smartphones, laptop computers, AIoT, and other markets. The relevant products have entered the supply chains of brands including Xiaomi and Transsion.

(2) DRAM Modules

The DRAM modules produced by the Company based on its own original DRAM chips include DDR4 and DDR5 series, covering different product types including RDIMM, MRDIMM, UDIMM/CUDIMM, SODIMM/CSODIMM, and LPCAMM. Among these, RDIMM and MRDIMM are primarily used in servers; UDIMM/CUDIMM is primarily used in computer workstations and desktop computers; SODIMM/CSODIMM is primarily used in laptop computers and all-in-one computers; and LPCAMM is primarily used in high-performance laptop computers and mobile workstations. The Company's DDR4 modules are available in multiple capacity specifications including 8GB/16GB/32GB/64GB, with speeds up to 3,200 Mbps. DDR5 modules are available in multiple capacity specifications including 8GB/16GB/32GB/64GB/96GB, with speeds up to 5,600 Mbps and above. (II) The Issuer's Principal Business Model

1. R&D Model

The Company's technology and product R&D process is divided into stages including concept, planning, development, and verification. Specific details are as follows:

(1) Concept Stage: After this stage is initiated, the Company begins forming a team, defining platform and product requirements, and analyzing the feasibility and manufacturability of platform and product development. After the overall risks of the project's concept stage are assessed and approved through a comprehensive evaluation, the development team formulates a project milestone plan and submits it for review. Upon approval, the project enters the planning stage.

(2) Planning Stage: This stage primarily involves optimizing the project quality objectives and plans, product development plans, financial assessments, and financial plans based on customer requirements. After the Technical Review Committee approves the risk assessment of the product development scope, specifications and schedule, development plan, and testing requirements, the development team submits the project plan for a decision review. Upon approval, the project enters the development stage.

(3) Development Stage: During this stage, the project R&D team designs the product architecture according to the R&D plan and product specifications, carries out a series of layout, circuit, packaging, and other designs, and verifies and confirms product functionality through simulation. After design rule checks and design reviews, the design data is released and tape-out (tapeout) is initiated. Upon successful completion, the project enters the verification stage.

(4) Verification Stage: The verification stage is primarily divided into two parts: engineering verification and customer verification.

  1. Engineering Verification involves completing internal testing and external platform testing in accordance with international JEDEC standards, verifying reliability and system compatibility. During this stage, the relevant departments begin monitoring product verification activities, monitoring and managing configurations and changes, fixing program defects identified during engineering verification, and improving product yield. Upon the engineering samples meeting the required standards, the project enters the customer verification stage.
  2. Customer Verification involves executing customer sample submission plans, obtaining customer product certification, and improving quality levels. During this stage, the Company's R&D team fixes program defects identified during customer verification and improves product yield, while promptly tracking the project's execution status and analyzing technical risks. The Company evaluates whether to proceed with large-scale production based on market and customer development conditions, and upon submission and approval of the review, the project enters the mass production stage.

2. Procurement Model

During the reporting period, the Company's procurement activities primarily included equipment procurement, raw material procurement, back-end process procurement, engineering procurement, and general procurement. For different types of procurement, the Company has formulated corresponding procurement management systems and strictly implements the relevant regulations in the processes of supplier selection, evaluation, and quality control in order to improve production efficiency, reduce inventory accumulation, and strengthen cost control.

(1) Procurement Management

The Company has established a comprehensive procurement management system for end-to-end management from procurement application to receipt of goods and payment. The Company's main procurement process is as follows:

  1. Procurement Application: The requisitioning department submits the corresponding operating and capital expenditure review committee for review in accordance with the requirements, budgeted amounts, and the Company's requisition authorization limits. After the review committee's resolution is approved, the requisitioning department issues a procurement application.
  2. Procurement Contracts/Purchase Orders: The procurement department selects suitable suppliers through methods such as public tender, invited tender, direct appointment, single-source procurement, and inquiry-based procurement based on the approved procurement application, and determines the winning/selected supplier through technical and commercial evaluations. The procurement department then enters into a procurement contract with the winning/selected supplier and creates a purchase order. After the purchase order is approved, the procurement officer sends the approved purchase order to the supplier and proceeds with the purchase.
  3. Receipt and Payment: The requisitioning department or warehouse accepts and receives the purchased products and services, and the finance department completes payment in accordance with the payment application and the terms agreed in the contract or purchase order.

(2) Supplier Management

  1. New Supplier Admission

    The Company has established a comprehensive supplier admission mechanism that standardizes the full-process evaluation of new suppliers from procurement requirement application, sourcing, and qualification review through to onboarding. The procurement department conducts sourcing and development of new suppliers based on anticipated future procurement needs. New suppliers must undergo comprehensive reviews by multiple departments. For suppliers that pass the review and are deemed applicable, they must also complete on-site audits and sample verification. Only after passing all reviews in their entirety may they be added to the qualified supplier list.

  2. Supplier Performance Evaluation

    The Company conducts regular performance assessments of suppliers, evaluating important suppliers periodically from the dimensions of quality, delivery, cost, technology, and service. For projects where evaluation results fail to meet standards, suppliers must provide improvement plans and complete improvements within the specified timeframe. Suppliers that fail to complete rectification or fail to meet standards consecutively are subject to measures such as reduction of transactions, freezing, or elimination.

  3. Supplier Management and Exit

    The Company has established a comprehensive mechanism and process for supplier freezing, elimination, and blacklisting, dynamically maintains the qualified supplier list, optimizes and upgrades the supply chain, and inspects the operational status of outsourcing vendors in accordance with regular audit plans.

3. Production Model

The semiconductor industry chain primarily consists of the main segments of chip design, wafer fabrication, and packaging and testing. Given the long production chain and numerous production processes involved in actual semiconductor production, the Company has comprehensively considered factors such as its own manufacturing process capabilities, capacity utilization, production efficiency, and cost-effectiveness, and has chosen to use outsourced processing for the chip packaging segment and certain finished product testing and module processing and testing segments. In the semiconductor industry, some semiconductor companies outsource portions of their production while also possessing their own manufacturing resources; the Company's adoption of outsourced production is in line with industry practice.

The main processes in the wafer fabrication, chip packaging, finished product testing, and module processing and testing segments of the DRAM product manufacturing process are as follows:

(1) Wafer Fabrication Segment

The Company's production process can be divided into the following segments: formulating production plans, compiling work plans, confirming work preparation and executing production, process monitoring and control, and wafer output and warehousing.

The Company's planning department comprehensively considers production factors and, in conjunction with product sales demand plans, capacity plans, and relevant production parameters provided by the relevant departments, reaches a consensus by organizing relevant departments to participate in parameter review meetings, thereby forming a regular wafer input-output plan. The manufacturing department arranges product sequencing and production line sequence in accordance with the production plan and based on the actual production line indicators, and confirms whether the relevant preparation work of each department is in place according to the work plan. After production preparations are complete, the manufacturing department executes production tasks in accordance with the work plan. The manufacturing department monitors the production line status in accordance with production indicators, and conducts measurement and monitoring of process quality and product quality jointly with the engineering department and quality department.

(2) Chip Packaging and Finished Product Testing Segment

For packaging operations, the Company adopts an outsourced production model on the basis of independently developed technology, entrusting the packaging process to outsourced packaging and testing manufacturers for contracted production. The production department raises output requirements in accordance with the production plan; the planning department is responsible for formulating outsourced production plans in accordance with the Company's relevant standards; and the outsourcing management business department is primarily responsible for evaluating and onboarding new outsourcing vendors or new projects, entering into contracts, following up on order delivery deadlines, and verifying invoices.

For testing operations, the Company adopts a production model primarily based on independent testing supplemented by outsourced testing, with the independent testing on the basis of self-developed technology. A portion of the testing operations is completed by the wholly-owned subsidiary Chang Xin Products Hefei (长鑫产品合肥), and a portion is outsourced to packaging and testing manufacturers. The production department raises output requirements in accordance with the production plan; the planning department is responsible for formulating comprehensive production plans in accordance with the Company's relevant standards and allocating them to the Company's independent testing facilities and each contracted testing vendor; and the outsourcing management business department is primarily responsible for evaluating and onboarding new outsourcing vendors or new projects, entering into contracts, following up on order delivery deadlines, and verifying invoices.

(3) Module Processing and Testing Segment

The Company's module processing and testing are primarily completed through outsourced processes. The Company's planning department is responsible for formulating outsourced production plans in accordance with the Company's relevant standards and allocating processing quantities to each outsourced manufacturer. The outsourcing management business department is primarily responsible for evaluating and onboarding new outsourcing vendors or new projects, entering into contracts, following up on order delivery deadlines, and verifying invoices.

The Company's distribution model is a buy-out sales model, and the distribution model is one of the Company's primary sales models. Distributors can help the Company rapidly establish sales channels, expand market share, achieve rapid turnover of products and funds, and improve the Company's operational efficiency and responsiveness.

(2) Distributor Management

In terms of distributor management, the Company has established a comprehensive distributor management system with strict controls over distributor introduction, management, and evaluation:

  1. Distributor Introduction: The Company comprehensively evaluates distributors in terms of their creditworthiness, financial strength, company size, industry standing, human resources, and service level, while also incorporating the cooperation intentions of end customers to select and introduce the best candidates.
  2. Distributor Evaluation: The Company typically enters into annual framework cooperation agreements with qualified distributors who have passed evaluation, stipulating matters such as the distribution cooperation model, payment methods, and internal control norms. The Company's relevant departments conduct regular evaluations of distributors from the dimensions of business performance, delivery, technical support, and market research. For distributors with unsatisfactory evaluation scores, the Company terminates or does not renew the cooperation agreement for the following year.

(III) Evolution of Principal Business, Main Products, and Primary Business Model Since Establishment

1. Principal Business and Primary Business Model

The Company operates under the IDM (Integrated Device Manufacturer) model. Under this model, enterprises can accumulate long-term and deep experience in R&D, production, and other aspects, which significantly promotes the accumulation of technology and processes and the comprehensive improvement of the product portfolio. In addition, IDM enterprises have the advantage of internal resource integration; the cycle from chip design to completion of manufacturing is generally shorter, effectively ensuring rapid product iteration while also enabling efficient response to customer needs. However, the IDM model places high demands on an enterprise's R&D capabilities, financial strength, technical level, and operational management capabilities.

Since its establishment, the Company has been consistently dedicated to the R&D, design, production, and sale of DRAM products. The Company's principal business and primary business model have not undergone any material changes.

2. Evolution of Main Products

The Company continuously accelerates the iterative updating of its technology and services in accordance with market demand and the direction of technological development, and continuously launches diversified and market-competitive DRAM product portfolios. Since its establishment, the Company has rapidly launched DDR4, DDR5, LPDDR4X, and LPDDR5/5X series products. In the coming years, the Company will actively seize industry development trends, accelerate the enrichment and upgrading of existing products, launch more products with larger capacity, higher speed, and lower power consumption, and at the same time proactively plan and launch products of newer generations. (III) Development of the Industry to Which the Company Belongs

1. Overview of the DRAM Industry

The semiconductor industry, as the cornerstone of modern information technology and the digital economy, plays a vitally important role in global technological progress and economic development. From a functional perspective, semiconductors primarily encompass four major categories: integrated circuits, optoelectronic devices, discrete devices, and sensors.

Among these, memory chips, as a key branch of integrated circuits, are electronic devices that perform data storage, reading, and writing functions. In recent years, the total volume of global data has experienced explosive growth. As an important safeguard for the timely, complete, and reliable transmission of information in the digital era, memory chips are not only core components of electronic devices but also core supports enabling the realization and development of next-generation information technology.

Based on whether data is lost after a power outage, memory chips are classified into two categories: volatile memory and non-volatile memory. Volatile memory primarily refers to Random Access Memory (RAM), which requires continuous power supply to temporarily store data for reading, writing, and processing by the main system CPU. Non-volatile memory primarily includes Read-Only Memory (ROM) and other memory types that can retain data over the long term without continuous power supply.

(1) With the Vigorous Development of the Digital Economy, DRAM, as the Core Hardware Carrier for Data Reading, Writing, and Transmission, is One of the Single Product Categories with the Highest Market Share and Fastest Growth Rate in the Global Semiconductor Market

DRAM is the mainstream volatile semiconductor memory, primarily used for the temporary storage of data and programs. As the key bridge for data exchange between processors and external storage, DRAM is also commonly referred to as the memory chip, and is widely used in various electronic devices and systems including servers, mobile devices, personal computers, and intelligent vehicles.

Each storage cell in a DRAM consists of only one transistor and one capacitor (1T1C), representing data bits (0 or 1) through the charge and discharge states of the capacitor. Due to the natural leakage characteristics of capacitors, periodic charge refresh is required to maintain data integrity — hence the designation "dynamic memory." Compared to other volatile memories such as SRAM, DRAM cells are structurally simple, enabling the integration of a large number of storage cells within a limited chip area. DRAM possesses advantages in high storage capacity and cost efficiency, and therefore has broad application scenarios.

(2) Classification of DRAM Chips

In the course of DRAM development, DRAM product types have been primarily classified into DDR (Double Data Rate Synchronous Dynamic Random Access Memory), LPDDR (Low Power Double Data Rate Synchronous Dynamic Random Access Memory), GDDR (Graphics Double Data Rate Synchronous Dynamic Random Access Memory), and other new high-end memory types. Among these, DDR and LPDDR products accounted for the majority of market share in 2024. JEDEC has defined and established the aforementioned standardized memory technology specifications framework, enabling products designed by various manufacturers to meet the power, performance, and specification requirements of target applications, and ensuring interchangeability and compatibility.

Driven by multiple forces including technological breakthroughs, performance iteration, and market application demands, various types of DRAM products are exhibiting a trend of generational upgrades. Each new generation of products typically offers higher speed and capacity, as well as lower voltage and power consumption compared to the previous generation. The current mainstream DDR products are in the transition phase from DDR4 to DDR5; in 2024, DDR4 and DDR5 products achieved market shares of 45% and 52%, respectively, within the DDR product segment. In the LPDDR segment, LPDDR4 and LPDDR5 products achieved market shares of 46% and 53%, respectively, within the LPDDR segment in 2024.

In addition to the above classifications, in accordance with market convention, DRAM products can also generally be categorized as mainstream DRAM and niche DRAM. Mainstream DRAM is characterized by large capacity and high data transfer rates, and is primarily applied in market segments such as servers, mobile devices, and personal computers, where the downstream market features large scale and high technical requirements. Niche DRAM refers to products with relatively lower density and capacity, or products retired from mainstream DRAM specifications, and is primarily applied in application scenarios outside of mainstream markets, such as telecommunications, industrial control, and smart home.

(3) Analysis of the DRAM Industry Chain

From the perspective of the industry chain, the upstream of the DRAM industry chain includes IP, EDA, semiconductor equipment and components, and materials. The midstream of the industry chain includes DRAM design, wafer fabrication, and packaging and testing, which are the core segments of the industry chain. The downstream of the industry chain primarily encompasses application areas such as servers, mobile devices, personal computers, telecommunications, industrial control, and smart homes.

DRAM Industry Chain

Over many years, with the continuous refinement of the division of labor, midstream chip manufacturers in the industry chain have formed two principal business models: IDM (Integrated Device Manufacturer) and vertical specialization. Under the IDM model, enterprises typically independently complete the full process chain, including chip design, wafer fabrication, and packaging and testing, requiring enterprises to possess strong technological reserves and financial strength. Under the vertical specialization model, each segment is handled by different enterprises through specialized division of labor, whereby Fabless enterprises focus on product research and development and design, wafer fabrication is typically outsourced to

Manufacturers operating under the IDM model occupy an absolutely dominant position in the midstream segments of the DRAM industry chain. Given the high degree of integration between DRAM layout design and wafer fabrication technology, IDM manufacturers are able to advance technology upgrades and product iterations more rapidly, and respond to rapidly changing market demands. Furthermore, under the characteristics of large-scale standardized production of DRAM products, IDM manufacturers can easily achieve economies of scale through centralized resource allocation, yielding more significant economic benefits. Leading global DRAM manufacturers, including Samsung Electronics, SK Hynix, Micron Technology, Chang Xin Technology (长鑫科技), and Nanya Technology, all operate under the IDM model.

2. Cyclical Characteristics of the Industry

The DRAM industry is significantly affected by fluctuations in market supply and demand and exhibits pronounced cyclical characteristics. Manufacturers adjust production capacity in response to changes in downstream demand; however, the capacity ramp-up for newly constructed production lines typically requires multiple quarters, and the time from capital expenditure to capacity release for newly built wafer fabs can span as long as several years, resulting in a mismatch between market supply and demand. At the same time, given the high concentration of supply in the DRAM market — where leading manufacturers hold the overwhelming majority of market share — capital expenditures among the major players tend to be concentrated, and decisions to add or reduce capacity are often released simultaneously, causing the DRAM industry to exhibit stronger cyclicality compared to other semiconductor product industries.

DRAM products are in high market demand and are highly standardized, with similar specifications and performance across different DRAM manufacturers, giving them commodity-like characteristics. When supply and demand are mismatched, price competition in the market becomes particularly intense. At the same time, historically, multiple uncertain factors such as the pace of technology iteration, macroeconomic fluctuations, and trade policies have further exacerbated DRAM price volatility, resulting in significant fluctuations in DRAM market prices.

3. Technical Level and Characteristics of the Industry's Sub-Segment

(1) DRAM Products Are Based on Standard Technical Specifications, with Customized Extensions for Specific Application Scenarios

In order to build an interconnected DRAM industry ecosystem, JEDEC has formulated internationally applicable DRAM technical standards. By unifying key technical indicators such as interface protocols and timing parameters for memory chips and modules, these standards address compatibility issues at the foundational level, ensuring that DRAM products from different manufacturers can achieve compatibility at the application end. For example, the DDR5 standard — through unified data rates (e.g., 6,400 MT/s), dual-channel design, and other specifications — enables memory modules from different manufacturers to be used interchangeably with motherboards on a plug-and-play basis. Manufacturers can independently carry out internal architecture design and manufacturing process development to comply with these standardized technical specifications and launch standardized DRAM products.

In addition to standardized products for general-purpose scenarios, customized demand also exists in the market for specific application needs. Such customized DRAM products require manufacturers to undertake differentiated design to match the special requirements of end devices in terms of power consumption, dimensions, performance, and other aspects, thereby achieving a technological extension from general-purpose scenarios to specific scenarios, and supplementing niche demands that are difficult to cover by general standards.

(2) Products Continue to Evolve Toward New Generations with Faster Transfer Speeds, Greater Capacity, and Lower Power Consumption

As the continuous penetration of scenario digitalization drives the large-scale expansion of global data volumes, the demand for large-capacity, high-speed, low-latency memory to handle massive real-time data processing requirements continues to rise, and the market has a strong demand for higher-performance DRAM products. On the basis of continuous breakthroughs in semiconductor technology and processes, DDR and LPDDR products are continuously evolving toward new generations with faster transfer speeds, greater capacity, and lower power consumption. The market share of new-generation products such as DDR5 and LPDDR5 in markets including servers, mobile devices, and personal computers continues to climb.

1-1-134

At the same time, the continuously developing next-generation information technology has placed higher demands on data throughput, and major manufacturers continue to explore new DRAM architectures and manufacturing/packaging processes, driving DRAM products to evolve toward faster transmission speeds, larger capacities, and lower power consumption.

(3) Economic Benefits Drive Continuous Iteration and Innovation in DRAM Process Technology

Over the long term, the advancement of process nodes has been an important means for DRAM manufacturers to reduce costs and improve product performance. Under more advanced process nodes, DRAM cell sizes shrink, enabling more memory cells to be integrated per unit area, and the storage cost per bit is significantly reduced. At the same time, the reduction in transistor pitch effectively shortens the electron travel path, and the voltage requirements also decrease correspondingly, thereby achieving lower power consumption. Driven by multiple factors such as the rapid growth in data volume and continuous technological application innovation, users' demands for storage capacity, transmission speed, and power consumption continue to increase. Major manufacturers continuously shrink process nodes so that unit memory chips have higher capacity, faster transmission speeds, and lower power consumption. While significantly reducing unit costs, this also effectively improves product performance and generates economic benefits.

Due to physical and commercial challenges such as leakage current and cost, the evolution of planar DRAM architecture is approaching its limits, and the pace of process node shrinkage is slowing down. New architectures such as 4F² (i.e., 4F Square, a cell structure technology) further reduce cell area by vertically stacking transistors and capacitors, representing an important technological path for the further development of DRAM.

4. Major Barriers to Entry in This Industry

(1) Technological Barriers

DRAM has extremely high technological thresholds, with complex design and manufacturing processes involving a wide range of specialized fields. In the circuit design stage, DRAM design requires precisely balancing multiple stringent objectives—including high density, high transmission speed, low power consumption, and high reliability—within high-density arrays, and the design verification process is complex with a long cycle. In the manufacturing stage, it is necessary to continuously iterate toward more advanced process technologies to remain competitive. This process involves hundreds of complex and precise steps including photolithography, etching, thin-film deposition, diffusion and ion implantation, chemical cleaning, and chemical mechanical polishing (CMP), each of which has an important impact on the final product performance. Strict quality control must be implemented to ensure that the final chip products have good performance and stable operation.

At the same time, driven by the rapidly evolving demands of downstream applications, the DRAM industry features rapid technological iteration. This requires companies in the industry to possess strong technology reserves and R&D responsiveness, enabling them to continuously iterate products with higher storage density, lower power consumption, and faster read/write speeds in response to changes in downstream application markets. In this process, as process nodes continue to shrink, circuit design complexity increases, and the difficulty of core process steps such as photolithography precision, etch aspect ratios, and thin-film deposition grows geometrically. The high technological threshold forces new entrants into the industry to undergo a lengthy period of accumulation before achieving the design and manufacturing capabilities to respond quickly to the market, making technological barriers significant.

(2) Capital and Production Capacity Barriers

Major DRAM companies in the industry typically operate under the IDM model, requiring continuous and substantial capital investment simultaneously in technology iteration, product development, and production line construction to support business development. On one hand, the capital expenditure required to build new DRAM wafer fabrication lines is extremely high, with the investment scale for a single fab already exceeding tens of billions of US dollars. As process technology advances to more advanced nodes, the corresponding R&D costs, facility construction, and equipment investment will further escalate, posing stringent challenges to companies' financial strength. On the other hand, downstream applications continue to raise their performance requirements for DRAM products, and competition in the mainstream market is highly dependent on technological advancement. Companies must continuously invest large amounts of capital in technology R&D to maintain a foothold in the DRAM sector. Whether a company can keep pace with or lead the technological process is one of the key factors in maintaining market competitiveness in the DRAM field.

Under the extremely high capital expenditure requirements, DRAM manufacturers must scale production capacity to a sufficiently large magnitude in order to spread costs through economies of scale and generate economic benefits. As production capacity continues to expand, unit costs show a significant downward trend, and companies will have more ample profit margins. Leading companies can leverage scale advantages to continuously expand their market share and consolidate their existing market positions. The aforementioned capital and production capacity barriers significantly restrict the entry of potential competitors into the DRAM industry and drive continued increases in industry concentration.

(3) Talent Barriers

The DRAM industry is a typical talent-intensive industry, placing extremely high demands on the knowledge base, R&D capabilities, and practical experience of core talent. Currently, major DRAM manufacturers in the industry all operate under the IDM model, covering multiple segments including semiconductor design, manufacturing, and packaging and testing, which places higher demands on the breadth and depth of the talent pool. China's integrated circuit industry is in a stage of rapid development, with significant demand for high-end talent with technical expertise and experience, making the aggregation and accumulation of talent an important barrier to entry for new market participants.

(4) Operational Management Barriers

DRAM supply chain management is complex, and corporate operations and management are highly specialized and complex:

First, DRAM production and manufacturing involves hundreds of process steps from wafer start to packaging and testing, each of which requires precise control. This requires the operations management department to possess highly specialized knowledge, extensive operational experience, and refined scheduling capabilities to ensure the smooth operation of the entire production process.

Second, the DRAM production and manufacturing process involves a very large number of process steps and is highly complex, placing extremely high demands on quality monitoring, issue traceability, and yield control, often requiring the joint participation of multiple departments including R&D, process engineering, product, and testing. At the same time, a rigorous, comprehensive, and effective testing program is needed to ensure product quality.

At the same time, the DRAM production and manufacturing process requires a large variety of equipment and raw materials, relying on a large number of highly specialized equipment and raw material suppliers. These suppliers not only need to meet extremely high technical requirements but also need to guarantee supply stability. Meanwhile, downstream customers place high demands on on-time delivery and long-term stable supply. Therefore, companies must possess excellent full-industry-chain ecosystem coordination capabilities to achieve efficient operations across all segments from material procurement and production manufacturing to product delivery.

In addition, the DRAM industry belongs to the capital-intensive sector, and under high capital expenditures, efficient management is required to improve operational efficiency and achieve refined cost control. In this context, DRAM companies need to accumulate large-scale production and operations management capabilities over long-term production and business operations.

In summary, building an efficient operations management system requires highly specialized knowledge and deep experiential accumulation. For new companies planning to enter the industry, the gap in knowledge and management experience constitutes an important entry barrier that cannot be overcome in the short term.

5. Opportunities and Risks Faced

(1) Opportunities Faced

1) The Digital Economy Wave Develops Vigorously, Bringing a New Round of Growth Opportunities for the DRAM Market

Under the wave of the digital economy, data processing volumes are growing exponentially. In recent years, with the maturation and proliferation of next-generation information technology, industries are accelerating their move toward comprehensive digitalization, and digital transformation has become an important means to improve efficiency, reduce costs, and expand markets. Against this backdrop, the total global volume of data is experiencing explosive growth. According to IDC forecasts, the world will generate 213.56 ZB of data in 2025, growing more than twofold to 527.47 ZB by 2029.

The real-time reading and writing and efficient processing of massive data cannot be achieved without the core support of DRAM chips. As a key component responsible for temporary data storage in various electronic devices, DRAM not only has an important impact on the speed and efficiency of data processing, but is also the fundamental hardware for meeting the high-throughput, low-latency data demands of the digital economy era. The demand for access to and reading and writing of massive data has further catalyzed a broad market space for DRAM chips, bringing historically significant development opportunities for the Company's products.

2) In the International Competitive Environment, the Development of the Domestic Industry Chain Ecosystem Drives Domestic DRAM Manufacturers into a Strategic Window of Opportunity

Domestic mobile device, personal computer, smart vehicle, and other terminal brand manufacturers, based on the enormous demand of the Chinese market, continue to innovate and iterate their products, enhance product competitiveness, and hold an increasingly important share in the global market. At the same time, as international trade frictions have further intensified in recent years, many domestic customers in the downstream industry chain have become increasingly focused on supply chain security and stability, and domestic terminal brands are actively promoting the construction of localized supply chain systems. The rise of domestic terminal manufacturers and their demand for industry chain ecosystem development have brought enormous opportunities for domestic DRAM manufacturers.

3) The Slowdown of Moore's Law and New Technology Demands Grant Domestic DRAM Manufacturers a Catch-Up Window

Currently, internationally leading DRAM companies have entered a bottleneck in process node shrinkage. The dramatic cost increases and approaching physical limits in new product development have led to a significant increase in process difficulty, and DRAM manufacturers are actively exploring new technical solutions. However, the commercial validation of technological paths still carries uncertainty. While some technical solutions have shown promise in experimental stages, they still require long-term validation in areas such as yield control in mass production, cost compatibility, and compatibility with existing industry chains, and there is uncertainty regarding technological implementation.

In terms of R&D in new technology fields, domestic manufacturers and the top three international manufacturers are all in an exploratory stage. Domestic manufacturers are expected to seize the opportunity for industrial transformation and achieve rapid development opportunities, overcoming the competitive challenges they face in catching up with international leading levels. By focusing on technological breakthroughs and innovating products and technologies, they can gradually narrow the gap with international leading levels, and may even achieve leapfrog advancement in certain technologies and products.

(2) Risks Faced

1) Manufacturer Operating Risks Arising from Industry Cyclical Fluctuations

The DRAM industry has strong cyclical characteristics, with significant price volatility. As a typical capital-intensive industry, DRAM typically takes several years from planning to full production. When market demand surges due to technological iteration or the explosion of emerging applications, manufacturers cannot quickly increase capacity, leading to short-term product supply shortages that rapidly drive up product prices. However, the subsequent concentrated capacity expansion by leading manufacturers often leads to supply gluts, sending the industry into a downward cycle. At the same time, factors such as macroeconomic fluctuations and the capacity regulation strategies of major manufacturers in the highly concentrated market further exacerbate cyclical fluctuations.

Under the industry's strongly cyclical characteristics, unpredictable price fluctuations may cause DRAM product selling prices to rise or fall sharply in the short term, increasing the operational difficulty for industry manufacturers in production planning, inventory management, and capital expenditure decision-making, and placing certain pressure on their operating performance. The aforementioned uncertainties mean that manufacturers in the industry face operational challenges arising from the industry's strongly cyclical characteristics.

2) Supply Chain Instability Risks Arising from Continued Escalation of International Geopolitical Tensions

Continued escalation of international geopolitical tensions has brought potential instability risks to the globally distributed DRAM supply chain. Key segments of the DRAM supply chain span multiple countries and regions. If geopolitical tensions lead to adjustments in trade policies in certain regions, strengthened technology export controls, etc., this may affect the collaborative efficiency of various segments of the supply chain.

China's semiconductor and integrated circuit industry has a relatively short development history and relatively low industrial maturity. In recent years, the international political environment has become increasingly complex, with some countries adopting various means including but not limited to raising tariffs and restricting imports and exports to implement trade protectionism, limiting the development of China's high-tech industries represented by the semiconductor integrated circuit industry, bringing supply chain instability risks to the development of related industries in China.

3) China's DRAM Industry Started Relatively Late and Faces Certain Challenges in Short-Term Market Competition

The development of Chinese DRAM companies started relatively late compared to internationally leading companies, and there is still room for improvement in the depth and breadth of technology accumulation. International leading manufacturers have undergone decades of technological iteration and have formed deep accumulations in materials application, patent portfolios, and other areas; whereas China's relevant industry has had a relatively short period of independent exploration, and in areas such as advanced process R&D and key technology breakthroughs, more time is still needed for experience accumulation and system building, and the supporting capabilities of certain core technology segments also need to be further consolidated.

In terms of production capacity scale, the top three international manufacturers have leveraged long-term capacity expansion and integration to form significant economies of scale, dominating the global market. In comparison, China's DRAM industry is still in a development stage, with market share still in the process of continuous expansion.

6. The Position and Role of the Issuer's Industry in the Industry Chain and Its Correlation with Upstream and Downstream Industries

As an integrated R&D, design, and manufacturing company, the Company occupies the core midstream segment of the DRAM supply chain. For a detailed analysis of the DRAM supply chain, please refer to "(3) Development of the Industry to Which the Company Belongs" – "1. Overview of the DRAM Industry" – "(3) DRAM Supply Chain Analysis" in this section.

Leveraging continuous capacity construction and technological breakthroughs, the Company has formed critical driving and synergistic effects on the upstream and downstream of the DRAM supply chain. Looking upstream, the Company's large-scale production requirements provide important technology validation and commercialization application opportunities for upstream companies in equipment, materials, and other sectors, driving upstream supply chains to build more mature market supply capabilities. Looking downstream, the Company provides stable supply to downstream terminal customers in servers, mobile devices, personal computers, smart vehicles, and other sectors, helping downstream companies respond more flexibly to market demands, thereby facilitating the continued development and upgrading of downstream industry clusters. As a company occupying the core segment of the DRAM supply chain, the Company provides important support for the further improvement of the DRAM supply chain ecosystem.

III. Industry Position and Competitive Strengths and Weaknesses of the Issuer

(I) Overview of Major Companies in the Same Industry

The Company is the largest, most technologically advanced, and most comprehensively positioned integrated DRAM R&D, design, and manufacturing company in China. The major DRAM companies in the industry are as follows:

1. Samsung Electronics (005930.KS)

Samsung Electronics was established in 1969 and is headquartered in South Korea. It was listed on the Korea Stock Exchange in 1975 and is an internationally leading manufacturer and seller of electronic products. Samsung Electronics has divisions including the Device eXperience (DX) Division, the Device Solutions (DS) Division, and Samsung Display (SDC), among others. The DS Division primarily provides semiconductor-related products or services, mainly including semiconductor products such as DRAM, NAND, and mobile application processors, as well as wafer foundry services. In fiscal year 2024, Samsung Electronics achieved total operating revenue of KRW 3,008,709.03 billion and net profit of KRW 344,513.51 billion.

2. SK Hynix (000660.KS)

SK Hynix was established in 1983 and is headquartered in South Korea. It was listed on the Korea Stock Exchange in 1996 and is a company primarily engaged in the R&D, design, production, and sales of semiconductor memory, with main products including DRAM, NAND Flash, and other memory chips. In fiscal year 2024, SK Hynix achieved total operating revenue of KRW 661,929.60 billion and net profit of KRW 197,969.02 billion.

3. Micron Technology (MU.O)

Micron Technology was established in 1978 and is headquartered in the United States. It was listed on the NASDAQ Stock Exchange in 1984 and is a company engaged in the production and sales of semiconductor memory, providing DRAM, NAND Flash, NOR Flash, and other memory products. In fiscal year 2024, Micron Technology achieved total operating revenue of USD 25.111 billion and net profit of USD 778 million.

4. Nanya Technology (2408.TW)

Nanya Technology was established in 1995 and is headquartered in Taiwan, China. It was listed on the Taiwan Stock Exchange in 2000 and focuses on the R&D, design, manufacturing, and sales of DRAM. In fiscal year 2024, Nanya Technology achieved total operating revenue of TWD 34.132 billion and net loss of TWD 5.083 billion.

(II) Basis for Selection of Comparable Listed Companies

In terms of products and business, the major companies in the DRAM industry are overseas companies; therefore, the aforementioned global major DRAM manufacturers—Samsung Electronics, SK Hynix, Micron Technology, and Nanya Technology—are selected as comparable companies.

The Company is an integrated DRAM R&D, design, and manufacturing company. Currently, there are no listed companies on the A-share market with comparable products and businesses to the Company. Based on core factors such as technical characteristics, market position, and business model, TSMC, SMIC, and China Resources Micro are further selected as comparable companies, with details as follows:

1. TSMC (2330.TW)

TSMC was established in 1987 and is headquartered in Taiwan, China. It was listed on the Taiwan Stock Exchange in 1994, with its principal business including manufacturing and sales of integrated circuits and other semiconductor chips, packaging and testing, computer-aided design, and photomask manufacturing and other foundry services. According to TrendForce statistics, TSMC is the world's highest-revenue wafer foundry company. In fiscal year 2024, TSMC achieved total operating revenue of TWD 2,894.308 billion and net profit of TWD 1,172.432 billion.

2. SMIC (688981.SH)

SMIC (Semiconductor Manufacturing International Corporation) was established in 2000 and is headquartered in Shanghai, China. It was listed on the STAR Market of the Shanghai Stock Exchange in 2020, providing 8-inch and 12-inch wafer foundry and technology services to customers worldwide. SMIC is one of the world's leading integrated circuit wafer foundry companies and also the leader in integrated circuit manufacturing in mainland China. In fiscal year 2024, SMIC achieved total operating revenue of RMB 57.796 billion and net profit of RMB 5.373 billion.

3. China Resources Micro (688396.SH)

China Resources Micro was established in 2003 and is headquartered in Wuxi, Jiangsu Province, China. It was listed on the STAR Market of the Shanghai Stock Exchange in 2020, and is a semiconductor company with integrated full-industry-chain operational capabilities including chip design, mask manufacturing, wafer manufacturing, and packaging and testing. Its products focus on power semiconductors, intelligent sensors, and intelligent control. In fiscal year 2024, China Resources Micro achieved total operating revenue of RMB 10.119 billion and net profit of RMB 662 million.

Changxin Technology (长鑫科技) is an important competitor in the global mainstream DRAM market and is also the largest, most technologically advanced, and most comprehensively positioned integrated DRAM R&D, design, and manufacturing company in China. The Company launched its independently designed and produced 8Gb DDR4 product in September 2019, achieving a breakthrough "from zero to one" for mainland China's DRAM industry. Based on Omdia's calculation of global manufacturer DRAM sales in the second quarter of 2025, the Company's global market share is 3.97%, ranking fourth globally and first in China.
